skill.name: nano-banana-edit
skill.description: Guides image-to-image editing with Google Nano Banana 2 on RunComfy. It explains when to use the model, how to prepare prompts, and how to run the RunComfy CLI safely.
skill.summary: Edit and batch-process images with Nano Banana 2 through the RunComfy CLI.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, URL, filesystem, and blocker patterns, but most are Markdown examples or documented service endpoints. The confirmed risk is legitimate but meaningful: the skill directs a local RunComfy CLI to send prompts and image URLs to a third-party API and write outputs locally. No evidence found of malicious intent, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or hidden executable code in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Sends Image Data to RunComfy
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill instructs agents to run the RunComfy CLI for Nano Banana edits. This is core functionality, but prompts and image URLs are sent to an external model API.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented invocation and API behavior are explicit. The risk is confirmed data sharing with a third-party service, not evidence of malware.

medium_findings.title: Local Token and Output File Handling
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill documents token storage in a hidden home-directory config path and writes generated files to an output directory. This is expected for a CLI service, but it requires normal credential and path hygiene.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths and token location are directly documented. I did not find evidence that the skill reads unrelated files or bypasses permissions.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Trigger Static Alerts
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. Many command-execution alerts are inline code, tables, or fenced examples in documentation. They are not hidden scripts, automatic hooks, or executable files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: SKILL.md is documentation, and the flagged backticks appear in Markdown examples. The only real execution happens when the user or agent runs the documented CLI.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal and Cryptography Alerts Are Benign Text
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The path traversal alerts are placeholder HTTPS image URLs using ellipses. The weak cryptography alerts match descriptive text, not cryptographic code or unsafe hashing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines are Markdown prose, endpoint examples, and an exit-code table. I did not find code that performs traversal or cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documented Service Endpoints
low_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW. The hardcoded URLs point to RunComfy documentation, the model API, and example image inputs. They are expected for this skill, but users should understand the external service dependency.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The destination domains are openly documented and align with the skill purpose. I did not find callbacks, telemetry endpoints, or unrelated exfiltration domains.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Directed External Command Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on a local CLI command that performs remote model calls. This pattern should remain visible to users because it can transmit prompt and image data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command and remote API behavior are explicitly described. It is a legitimate integration pattern with privacy and execution implications.

content.user_title: Edit Images with Nano Banana on RunComfy
content.value_statement: Image editing workflows often lose subject identity or batch consistency. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical Nano Banana edit guidance through RunComfy.
actual_capabilities: Explains when Nano Banana Edit is the right model for image-to-image work.
Provides RunComfy CLI invocation patterns for single-image and batch edits.
Documents input fields including prompt, image URLs, resolution, aspect ratio, and seed.
Gives prompt guidance for identity preservation, background swaps, and localized edits.
Compares Nano Banana Edit with GPT Image 2 edit, Flux Kontext, and text-to-image alternatives.
Documents RunComfy token handling, outbound endpoints, and generated output behavior.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI, a RunComfy account, and valid authentication.
Sends prompts and image URLs to RunComfy model infrastructure for processing.
Needs publicly fetchable HTTPS image URLs for model inputs.
Long compound edit prompts can drift and may need multiple passes.

use_cases.title: Product Gallery Background Updates
use_cases.description: Create consistent product images with locked framing, aspect ratio, and resolution across multiple SKUs.

use_cases.title: Creator Portrait Variations
use_cases.description: Preserve a person, pose, and clothing while changing backgrounds or visual settings.

use_cases.title: Localized Object Edits
use_cases.description: Use spatial instructions to remove or change one image region while keeping the rest stable.

prompt_templates.title: Simple Background Swap
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Nano Banana Edit to keep the subject identity unchanged and replace only the background with a clean studio setting.

prompt_templates.title: Product Batch Consistency
prompt_templates.prompt: Edit this product image set with the same prompt grammar, locked aspect ratio, and matching resolution for a consistent catalog look.

prompt_templates.title: Precise Spatial Change
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Nano Banana Edit to change only the upper-right object. Keep all other objects, lighting, and camera framing unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Pass Creative Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a two-pass Nano Banana Edit workflow that first changes the background, then adjusts wardrobe while preserving face and pose.

output_examples.input: A request to replace a portrait background while keeping the person unchanged.
output_examples.output: A concise edit prompt, recommended RunComfy settings, and a note to avoid changing face, pose, or clothing.

output_examples.input: A batch of product images that need the same square studio presentation.
output_examples.output: A batch workflow with consistent prompt wording, fixed aspect ratio, fixed resolution, and expected output location guidance.

output_examples.input: A still-life image where only one object should be removed.
output_examples.output: A spatially scoped instruction that names the target object and explicitly preserves the remaining objects and lighting.

best_practices: State what must stay unchanged before describing the edit.
Use clear spatial language for local edits, such as background only or upper-right corner.
Lock aspect ratio and resolution when editing related images as a batch.
anti_patterns: Do not combine many unrelated edits in one prompt.
Do not use vague requests like make it better without preservation goals.
Do not send private or sensitive images unless RunComfy processing is acceptable.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It guides Nano Banana 2 image edits through the RunComfy CLI and explains practical prompt patterns.

faq.question: Does it generate images from scratch?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on image-to-image edits, not pure text-to-image generation.

faq.question: Can it edit many images at once?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented endpoint accepts up to 20 input images for batch or reference-based edits.

faq.question: What tools are required?
faq.answer: You need the RunComfy CLI, RunComfy authentication, and image URLs that the service can fetch.

faq.question: Is my image data sent outside my machine?
faq.answer: Yes. Prompts and image URLs are sent to RunComfy for remote model processing.

faq.question: When should I choose another model?
faq.answer: Use another model for stronger multilingual text editing, from-scratch generation, or very precise single-reference local edits.
